claim: Agent session memory is an ephemeral cache, never a store: project and work state is banned from it - the graph owns obligations, the repos own records, CLAUDE.md compiles the bootstrap. At session end, promote anything load-bearing through the write flow, then prune; what survives between sessions is at most a thin operator-preferences layer, and only until a private overlay collection gives preferences a graph home. The a386 doctrine applied to the agent's own notebook: a cache whose freshness is procedural embeds the staleness it was meant to solve.

evidence.detail: Proposed by the operator after two same-week incidents of memory rot under multi-session concurrency (the a367 stale-todo authoring and the bootstrap handoff failure, postscripts 4 and 6). Viable now because the week's promotions emptied memory of load: a504-a506, a497, a508, and the desk made every formerly memory-resident fact graph-resident. Applied immediately: the session's memory files were pruned to a single index line pointing at the graph.

First trial, same day: a memoryless 'begin' bootstrapped fully from the compiled ritual - pulled, read the desk, derived the freshest thread from transcript postscripts instead of memory, and caught the authoring session's own dangling a509 commit, which remembered state could never have surfaced. The orientation that memory used to provide was reconstructed from records at zero staleness risk. Anecdote grade: n=2 bootstraps, same operator.

# Composable Beliefs

A framework for giving AI agents persistent, source-grounded, inspectable reasoning that survives session boundaries and composes into understanding the agent never explicitly derived.

claim: Composable Beliefs is a reasoning and audit substrate, not a memory system: it layers over any memory/recall system rather than becoming one. Vector memory, model calls, recall/retrieval, and eval execution stay outside CB's scope and are owned by an external harness; CB ingests their outputs as observations and audits what they store and claim. Building retrieval into CB would forfeit the deterministic, LLM-free read path - the property the framework exists to provide.
Canonical enum of artifact URI schemes. Each scheme declared inline with its URI form. The enum is closed: no artifact value with a scheme outside this set is permitted on active beliefs.

definitions.code: An anchored site within a repository file. Form: code:<repo-relative-path>#<anchor>. Everything after the first '#' is one opaque literal substring anchor (the fixed-string grep target); an optional trailing '@<N>' selects the Nth match, and a literal trailing '@<digits>' is percent-encoded as '%40<digits>'. Line numbers are never stored - they resolve at render/run time. document: remains the whole-file reference.
definitions.document: A repository file reference. Form: document:<repo-relative-path>. Files under ops/, dates/, lib/, etc.
definitions.https: An external URL. Form: https:<URL-rest>. Web pages, papers, external resources.
definitions.session: A working session reference. Form: session:<date-or-descriptor>. Resolved via session transcript (persistence pipeline pending).
definitions.source: A cached source document reference. Form: source:<slug>. Documents stored under ops/sources/ or similar.
definitions.user: A direct user statement. Form: user:<name>:<date>. Internal authorship provenance.

invariants: Exactly one rule entry, with field 'artifact-scheme'.
Each value matches /^[a-z][a-z0-9-]*$/.
Values are unique within the entry.
Every value has a corresponding entry in the definitions map.
For all active beliefs b where b.artifact is not null: scheme(b.artifact) is in values.

No active belief used gmail: (schemes in use: session, document, user, plan, source, https), so removal strands nothing. The seven inherited schemes and all five invariants carry forward verbatim from cb:c043; single-scheme supersession consistent with a397's batching discipline.

claim: Decide whether to make the belief<->commit link structural by adding a commit:/git: artifact scheme to the closed enum (cb:c066), versus keeping it as the current unenforced prose convention. Today a git commit cannot be cited as a typed artifact from a belief at all: c066's schemes are session/user/document/source/https/plan/code, none for commits, so the only link is a literal id-string hand-typed into a commit subject (e.g. 'implements cb:a518'), grep-able but unverified, and the belief->commit direction has no link. This leaves the bottom rung of the audit chain (cb:a442: code->contract->graph->plan->raw conversation) untyped for change-events. ADVANTAGE of going structural: a directive marked done could carry commit:<sha> (or git:<repo>@<sha>), a resolver could dereference it (git show), and CI could enforce the loop both ways (a discrete directive flipping to done must cite a real commit; a commit claiming to implement cb:aNNN must name a live belief). It is the temporal analog of the code: scheme (which anchors a single site in a file; commits are content-addressed, so unlike code:'s line numbers the reference is stable). OPTIONS: (1) full commit:/git: scheme - a c066->c067 enum-registry supersession plus resolver, docs, tests, and the cb.repoint dep-swing; heaviest, but types both directions. (2) git-trailer half-measure - a 'Belief: cb:aNNN' trailer parsed by a hook into a generated commit<->belief index; no schema change, gives structural linkage from the commit side only. (3) invest in the session: pipeline instead (cb:a518/cb:a540) - a commit is a derived artifact of a session, so session resolution may be the higher-leverage bottom-rung work, with commit: a later narrow add. KEY JUDGEMENT: the scheme alone buys nothing; the value is unlocked only by enforcement/resolution, so the decision is really 'do we want CI-enforced done<->commit provenance?' If citation stays advisory, do nothing or just (2). Deferred at the operator's instruction (2026-06-22) pending that call.

claim: Generate the live-command transcript blocks in the eval worked example (docs/worked-example-eval-verdict.md) from the graph instead of hand-maintaining them. The doc pastes mix bs / mix cb.verify output verbatim, so it drifts whenever the artifact-scheme enum, the method: collection, or the sdl collection changes - this thread refreshed it twice (the verify counts, then the c043->c066 supersession section). Hand-pasted command output whose freshness depends on remembering to regenerate it is the a386 antipattern; a generator, or a doctest-style check that fails CI when a block goes stale, would render it live.

evidence.detail: Authored in the worked-example transcript-drift thread; the arc (the doc refreshed twice in one thread, c043->c066 being the second pass) and the generate-not-hand-maintain rationale are narrated in the chronicle.

Tradeoffs between the two paths the claim names, recorded so the choice need not be re-derived. GENERATOR (a mix task renders the doc's command blocks live from the graph): prevents drift outright since the committed doc is produced rather than hand-edited, but it must reproduce the doc's editorial curation rather than raw command dumps - blocks elide rows (e.g. '15 more schema PASS rows ... elided') and truncate claim columns - and it couples doc generation to the mix tooling plus a sibling belief-collections checkout, because the sdl/method blocks need --beliefs paths resolvable. CHECKER (a CI gate re-runs each fenced block and fails on mismatch): lighter, changes no authoring, makes silent drift impossible because the build goes red - but it only flags, a human still re-pastes, and it must normalize the curated/elided regions before diffing and resolve the cross-repo collections in CI. HYBRID is viable: generate verbatim-dump blocks, normalize-and-check the curated ones. Leaning (not decided): start with the checker - lowest authoring risk, stops drift immediately, the same normalization handles curation - and promote to a generator only if curation reproduction and cross-repo resolution prove cheap. Either path first needs belief-collections present in CI; that cross-repo dependency is the shared prerequisite.
